sources.notes: Statement and constraints: simulate f(k,a,l,r) over many range queries, with a_i and k bounded by 1e5, total n <= 1e5 and total q <= 5e4.
Official editorial notes that k changes only at positions where a[i] is a divisor of current k, and each divisor value can cause at most one effective change.
Maspy writeup cross-checks the direct occurrence-vector approach: enumerate divisors of k and binary-search positions of each divisor to find the next division point.
Chinese writeup gives a threshold/sqrt-decomposition variant with next-position tables for small divisors and occurrence vectors for large divisors.

annotation.summary: For each query (k,l,r), run the given loop over a[l..r], repeatedly dividing k by a[i] while possible and adding the current k after every index, but answer all queries efficiently.
annotation.constraints: a_i,k <= 1e5, total n <= 1e5, total q <= 5e4. A direct O(length) simulation per query is too slow.
annotation.core_idea: The value of k only changes at indices i where a[i] divides the current k. Between two such indices, k is constant, so the contribution is k times the length of that skipped segment. Precompute all divisors up to 1e5 and store the sorted positions of each value appearing in the array. For a query, at current position x enumerate all divisors d of the current k, lower_bound in pos[d] to find the first occurrence at or after x, and take the minimum such position y. Add k*(y-x). If y is inside r, divide k by a[y] until it is no longer divisible and continue from y; otherwise finish by adding the tail. The loop is short because every effective step strictly decreases k by at least a factor 2 after removing all copies of the divisor.
annotation.complexity: About O(changes * tau(k) * log n) per query with changes <= O(log k); preprocessing divisors costs O(V log V) for V=1e5
tricks: Skip maximal stretches where k does not change.
Find the next changing position by checking occurrence lists of current divisors.
Remove all copies of a divisor at the chosen position at once.
Use the bound on repeated divisions of k to keep the simulation short.

solution_variants.name: divisor-occurrence-jump-simulation
solution_variants.summary: Precompute divisor lists and positions for every array value. During a query, enumerate divisors of current k and binary-search their next occurrence to jump directly to the next index that can change k.
solution_variants.complexity: O(changes * tau(k) * log n) per query

solution_variants.name: small-large-divisor-threshold
solution_variants.summary: For smaller divisors keep nxt[position][d], and for larger divisors keep position lists over multiples. This reduces binary searches and is a more optimized implementation variant.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nB + q log V (B + log n)) depending on threshold B

tags.evidence: The only positions that matter for the simulation are those whose a[i] divides the current k.
Efficient queries require precomputing the divisor list of every possible k up to 1e5.
For each divisor of current k, the next candidate index is found by lower_bound in the sorted occurrence list of that divisor.
The algorithm still brute-forces all divisors of the current k at each effective change, relying on small value bounds.
An optimized variant splits small and large divisors, using next-position tables for small values and lists for large values.
The query loop aggregates whole unchanged segments as k*(length) instead of simulating every index.
